title: Nonequilibrium charge-density-wave order beyond the thermal limit
classification_notes: GPT error: Unterminated string starting at: line 185 column 19 (char 11688)

paper_analysis.summary: This paper studies ultrafast charge-density-wave (CDW) melting and recovery in TbTe3 using time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (trARPES) and time-resolved X-ray diffraction (trXRD). The key finding is that CDW order can transiently re-form at electronic temperatures far above the equilibrium transition temperature, which the authors attribute to suppressed lattice fluctuations in a nonthermal phonon population; a time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau model reproduces the observed amplitude dynamics and transient potential-energy landscape.

figures.description: Schematic of the trARPES and grazing-incidence trXRD experimental geometry on TbTe3, including the layered crystal structure and pump-probe arrangement.
Symmetrized Fermi surface of TbTe3 at 100 K showing nested regions, CDW wave-vector connections, and tight-binding band contours.
figures.likely_source: experimental data with theoretical tight-binding overlays

figures.description: Representative X-ray Bragg peaks along the (3 7 L) direction before and after optical excitation, showing suppression of the CDW superlattice peak.
Tight-binding band structure along the momentum cut used for trARPES analysis, showing main bands, shadow bands, and hybridized CDW bands.
trARPES spectrum at pump-probe overlap showing the CDW gap and shadow bands.
trARPES spectrum 120 fs after excitation showing suppression of the CDW gap and shadow bands.
Energy distribution curves extracted from the trARPES cut.
Momentum distribution curves extracted from the trARPES cut.
Time evolution of inverted in-gap intensity for several fluences, compared with time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau simulations.
Time evolution of shadow-band intensity for several fluences.
Time evolution of the CDW superlattice peak intensity from trXRD for several fluences, with layered Ginzburg-Landau simulations.
High-fluence recovery dynamics of the inverted in-gap intensity over longer delays.
Inverted in-gap intensity plotted versus extracted electronic temperature, comparing static temperature dependence with the dynamic photoexcited trajectory.
Simulated transient potential energy surface and order-parameter trajectory in the amplitude-mode regime after weak excitation.
Simulated transient potential energy surface and order-parameter trajectory in the overshoot regime after strong excitation.
Three-temperature model showing electronic, hot-phonon, and lattice temperature evolution and the transient critical temperature.
Conceptual illustration of equilibrium metallic state, equilibrium CDW state, and nonthermal photoexcited CDW stabilization above Tc.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: Raw measurements include trARPES spectra collected with a laser-based HHG setup (21.7 eV probe, 1.55 eV pump) and trXRD diffraction intensities collected at the Swiss Light Source FEMTO hard X-ray slicing source (7 keV X-rays) under optical pumping. The paper also mentions static ARPES / temperature-dependent measurements used to establish equilibrium behavior and a static temperature series for the in-gap intensity proxy.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The paper states that the data supporting the findings are publicly available in Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4106272.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: The paper explicitly says the Zenodo deposit contains 'time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy data and time-resolved X-ray diffraction data of TbTe3'. The raw measurements are implied to be the trARPES spectra and trXRD diffraction data collected with the described instruments; no file formats are specified in the paper text.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data include extracted observables such as inverted in-gap intensity, shadow-band intensity, normalized superlattice peak intensity, and extracted electronic temperature from Fermi-Dirac fits. The paper also refers to static temperature-series data used to compare the order-parameter proxy against a BCS-like temperature dependence. No explicit table of values is described in the text.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Figure-specific data are clearly implied: time traces for multiple fluences, momentum cuts, energy/momentum distribution curves, diffraction peak fits, and temperature-dependent order-parameter plots. The paper does not explicitly say 'source data' files are provided, but the Zenodo repository is likely to contain the underlying data supporting the figures.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: The paper mentions time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau simulations and a layered Ginzburg-Landau model, plus a three-temperature model, but it does not mention releasing analysis scripts, notebooks, or code.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset appears to combine experimental raw measurements and derived quantities used for figure generation. The text distinguishes experimental observables from model outputs: trARPES and trXRD are measurements; inverted in-gap intensity, shadow-band intensity, electronic temperature, and normalized peak intensities are processed/extracted quantities; tdGL and 3TM curves are simulated/theoretical. The repository contents beyond the general description are unknown from the paper text.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The Data availability section says the findings are supported by publicly available 'time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy data and time-resolved X-ray diffraction data of TbTe3' in Zenodo.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: The figures rely on extracted quantities such as inverted in-gap intensity, shadow-band intensity, normalized superlattice peak intensity, and electronic temperature, which are processed from the raw measurements and are likely the figure-ready source data.
data_availability_section_relevant: The data that support the ﬁndings of this study are publicly available in Zenodo64 with the identiﬁer https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4106272.
